author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
Augustin Himself. Two Methods Whereby Sins, Like Diseases, are Guarded Against.
title: Augustin Himself. Two Methods Whereby Sins, Like Diseases, are Guarded Against.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Two Methods Whereby Sins, Like Diseases, are Guarded Against.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Let us now turn to our own case.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: “Bishop Augustin also,” says your author, “in his books on Free Will has these words: ‘Whatever the cause itself of volition is, if it is impossible to resist it, submission to it is not sinful; if, however, it may be resisted, let it not be submitted to, and there will be no sin.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Does it, perchance, deceive the unwary man?

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Let him then beware that he be not deceived.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Is the deception, however, so potent that it is not possible to guard against it?

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: If such is the case, then there are no sins.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: For who sins in a case where precaution is quite impossible?

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Sin, however, is committed; precaution therefore is possible.’”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: I acknowledge it, these are my words; but he, too, should condescend to acknowledge all that was said previously, seeing that the discussion is about the grace of God, which helps us as a medicine through the Mediator; not about the impossibility of righteousness.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Whatever, then, may be the cause, it can be resisted.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Now it is because of this that we pray for help, saying, “Lead us not into temptation,” and we should not ask for help if we supposed that the resistance were quite impossible.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: It is possible to guard against sin, but by the help of Him who cannot be deceived.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: For this very circumstance has much to do with guarding against sin that we can unfeignedly say, “Forgive us our debt, as we forgive our debtors.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Now there are two ways whereby, even in bodily maladies, the evil is guarded against,—to prevent its occurrence, and, if it happen, to secure a speedy cure.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: To prevent its occurrence, we may find precaution in the prayer, “Lead us not into temptation;” to secure the prompt remedy, we have the resource in the prayer, “Forgive us our debts.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Whether then the danger only threaten or be inherent, it may be guarded against.
